ORDER GRANTING PLAINTIFFS' AND CROSS-DEFENDANTS' MOTION TO TRANSFER AND CONSOLIDATE ACTIONS

LAW AND MOTION 302, PLAINTIFFS AND CROSS-DEFENDANTS STANFORD-STARTX FUND, ET AL.'S MOTION TO TRANSFER AND CONSOLIDATE ACTIONS IS GRANTED. THE CLAIMS ASSERTED BY DEFENDANT AND CROSS-COMPLAINANT ARTURO DEVESA IN DEVESA V. STANFORD UNIVERSITY, SANTA CLARA COUNTY SUPERIOR COURT CASE NO. 19-CV-347760, SHARE NUMEROUS COMMON FACTUAL AND LEGAL ISSUES WITH THOSE ASSERTED BY MR. DEVESA IN HIS SECOND AMENDED CROSS-COMPLAINT AND PROPOSED THIRD AMENDED CROSS-COMPLAINT IN THIS ACTION, AND INVOLVE MANY OF THE SAME NAMED PARTIES. MR. DEVESA'S CONTENTION IN HIS LATE-FILED OPPOSITION BRIEF THAT THE TWO ACTIONS ARE "NOT RELATED" IS MERITLESS. ALTHOUGH MR. DEVESA HAS NAMED ADDITIONAL INDIVIDUAL DEFENDANTS IN THE SANTA CLARA ACTION, THE UNDERLYING ALLEGATIONS AND CLAIMS OVERLAP SUBSTANTIALLY. PLAINTIFFS AND CROSS-DEFENDANTS HAVE SHOWN, AND THE COURT FINDS, THAT THE CASES MEET THE STANDARDS SPECIFIED IN SECTION 404.1 OF THE CODE OF CIVIL PROCEDURE, ARE NOT COMPLEX AS DEFINED BY THE JUDICIAL COUNCIL, AND THAT THE MOVING PARTIES HAVE MADE A GOOD FAITH EFFORT TO OBTAIN AGREEMENT TO THE TRANSFER FROM ALL PARTIES TO EACH ACTION. ACCORDINGLY, PURSUANT TO SECTION 403 OF THE CODE OF CIVIL PROCEDURE, THAT ACTION IS ORDERED TRANSFERRED TO THIS COURT, AND THE TWO ACTIONS ARE ORDERED CONSOLIDATED FOR ALL PURPOSES INCLUDING TRIAL. (CODE CIV. PROC. 403, 1048.) THE INSTANT ACTION SHALL BE THE LEAD ACTION, AND ALL FURTHER PLEADINGS SHALL BE FILED UNDER THIS CASE NUMBER. ORDER IS SIGNED IN OPEN COURT. JUDGE: ETHAN P. STRAHLENDORF (1992) 7 CAL.APP.4TH 1911, 1913 ["A MOTION TO STRIKE MUST BE FILED 'WITHIN THE TIME ALLOWED TO RESPOND TO A PLEADING ....' ( 435, SUBD. (B)(1).)"].) SECOND, THE GROUNDS FOR THE MOTION TO STRIKE MUST APPEAR ON THE FACE OF THE PLEADING OR ON MATTERS THE COURT CAN JUDICIALLY NOTICE. (ID.) THE MOTION FOR SANCTIONS IS DENIED BECAUSE DEFENDANT FAILED TO COMPLY WITH THE SAFE HARBOR PROVISION THAT ALLOWS A PARTY TO WITHDRAW AN OFFENDING PLEADING. (CODE CIV. PROC., 128.5(F)(1)(B).) DEFENDANT FAILS TO MAKE AN EVIDENTIARY SHOWING THAT PLAINTIFF'S COMPLAINT IS A FRIVOLOUS PLEADING THAT COMPLETELY LACKS MERIT OR WAS PRESENTED FOR A HARASSING PURPOSE. MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT .
Off Calendar Jan-28-2020 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: Appearances:
Bryan Ketroser, Esq. 415-830-5897 of Alto Litigation for Plaintiff.
No other Appearance.

RULING - The matter is Uncontested/Discussed. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT ARTURO DEVESA's MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT.

Defendant Devesa's motion for summary judgment is denied. First, the motion is procedurally improper because it is unclear against which plaintiff/s it is brought and precisely which claims Mr. Devesa seeks to adjudicate. The notice of motion and separate statement do not comply with CRC 3.1350(b) and (d). Second, on January 14, 2020, the court imposed issue sanctions against defendants and they therefore cannot contest plaintiffs' allegations. (See Code of Civ. Proc., 2033.410(a) [admissions conclusive].) To cite only one of many examples, Mr. Devesa contends that Massive Investment did in fact invest $400,000 in MedWhat. (Devesa's Undisputed Facts 3, 8, & 9.) Based on the discovery order, Mr. Devesa has now conclusively admitted that this assertion is false. (See January 14, 2020 Order Granting Motion to Impose Discovery Sanctions, 3:24-28.) Defendant's motion is accordingly denied.

Order is signed. (see scanned order for details)

Notice Of Motion And Motion To Impose Discovery Sanctions On Defendants Medwhat.Com And Arturo Devesa For Failure To Comply With Discovery Orders Cal. Civ. Proc. Sec. 2023.010 And 2023.030
Off Calendar Jan-14-2020 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: Appearances: Atty. Bryan Ketroser of ALTO Litigation 415-868-5602. appeared for Plaintiff. No other appearances.

The Court adopted its tentative ruling as follows: PLAINTIFF STANFORD-STARTX FUND, LLC's Motion To Impose Discovery Sanctions On Defendants Medwhat.Com And Arturo Devesa For Failure To Comply With Discovery Orders Cal. Civ. Proc. Sec. 2023.010 And 2023.030. Plaintiff's motion to impose terminating sanctions against defendants is denied. The alternative request seeking evidentiary and issue sanctions is granted. The motion is unopposed. The defendants failed to comply with two discovery orders dated June 25, 2019 and November 11, 2019. Each order required service of code-compliant responses for discovery initially served on February 26, 2019. Defendant Devasa also failed to appear at the November 11, 2019 to address deficiencies in his responses despite a direction to do so in the published Tentative Ruling. The responses defendant Devasa served were non-complaint with the earlier discovery order. Defendants have disregarded the code complaint requirements of the discovery statutes to the prejudice to plaintiff in its preparation for trial which warrants imposition of evidentiary and issue sanctions. Defendant Medwhat.Com., Inc. remains unrepresented by counsel notwithstanding Devesa's commitment at the June hearing that corporate counsel would be retained within three weeks. Noted is the order of November 11, 2019 further directed counsel for the corporation be retained within twenty days. The defendants' failure to retain corporate counsel placed Medwhat.Com, Inc. at risk for forfeiture of its rights through non-representation which is what has occurred. See: Ferruzzo v. Superior Court, 104 Cal. App. 3d 501 (1980). The written order to be entered will grant the evidentiary and issue sanctions against both defendants as detailed in plaintiff's Notice of Motion and will be set forth in the order. Monetary sanctions are awarded to plaintiff against defendants in the sum of $10,000.00 to be paid within twenty days of the date of this order with Notice of Entry. Order signed. Judge Pro Tem Steven Stein, Clerk K. Liu, not reported. (D302)

Off Calendar Sep-17-2019 Sustained
MINI MINUTES: Appearances:
Bryan Ketroser, Esq. 415-830-5897 of Alto Litigation for Plaintiff.
Nathalie Fayad, Esq. of Lewis & Llewelyn LLP for Cross-Defendant Suzanne Fletcher.

RULING - The matter is Discussed. The Court states there is no Appearance by Defendant Arturo Devesa inspite of the email the Court has received that he was appearing to contest the Court's tentative ruling. (as fully reported in the reporter's notes/transcript) The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

CROSS DEFENDANT SUZANNE FLETCHER'S DEMURRER TO ARTURO DEVESA'S THIRD AMENDED CROSS COMPLAINT.

Cross defendant Suzanne Fletcher's demurrer to the fraud claim is sustained without leave to amend. The claim still lacks the requisite particularity to plead a fraud claim. In addition, the claim makes little sense. Devasa is asserting fraud regarding how cross-defendants invested money in Medwhat. Cross-defendants are not alleged to have taken money from or otherwise caused damages to Devasa. It is also unclear how Fletcher's statements regarding the convertible notes harmed Devesa. (Third Amended Cross Complaint, 296.) Lastly, any alleged harm affected Medwhat and only Medwhat has standing to assert the claim. (Id. at 336.)

Cross defendant Suzanne Fletcher's demurrer to the defamation claim is sustained without leave to amend. The cross complaint fails to allege what specific libelous or slanderous statement Fletcher uttered. (See Gilbert v. Sykes (2007) 147 Cal.App.4th 13, 31 [" 'The general rule is that the words constituting an alleged libel must be specifically identified, if not pleaded verbatim, in the complaint.' "]; ZL Technologies, Inc. v. Does 1-7 (2017) 13 Cal.App.5th 603, 616 ["In defamation cases California follows a similar pleading rule, under which 'the words constituting an alleged libel must be specifically identified, if not pleaded verbatim, in the complaint.'"].) The cross-complaint lumps the movants together and generally alleges that they defamed Devasa's good name and spread a false narrative. (Third Amended Cross Complaint, 343 and 345.) While the complaint has more detail regarding cross defendant Boag (Third Amended Cross Complaint, 346), it is lacking as to Fletcher. Regent's and Fletcher's statement that Regent had not committed to its $3.2 million Series A investment is not defamatory of Devesa. (Third Amended Cross Complaint, 351 and 356.)

Order is signed. (see scanned order for details)

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman, Clerk: M. Goodman, Court Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR#8600, maria.torreano@gmail.com, Reported. =(302/EPS)

No other appearances made in this matter.

HEARING RE: DEFENDANT ARTURO DEVESA'S MOTION TO STRIKE CERTAIN PLAINTIFFS COUNTS AGAINST DEFENDANT UNDER CODE OF CIVIL PROCEDURE SEC. 435-437.

Matter submitted without argument. The Court adopts its tentative ruling as follows: Defendant Devesa's motion to strike is denied. First, the motion is untimely because defendant already answered the complaint. (See City and County of San Francisco v. Strahlendorf (1992) 7 Cal.App.4th 1911, 1913 ["A motion to strike must be filed 'within the time allowed to respond to a pleading ....' ( 435, subd. (b)(1).)"].) Second, the grounds for the motion to strike must appear on the face of the pleading or on matters the court can judicially notice. (Id.) Defendant must bring a motion for summary judgment if he wishes to contest the factual basis of plaintiff's allegations.

Matter submitted without argument. The Court adopts its tentative ruling as follows: Defendant Devesa's motion for sanctions is denied. This motion largely repeats defendant's procedurally improper motion to strike. Defendant fails to make an evidentiary showing that plaintiff's complaint is a frivolous pleading that completely lacks merit or was presented for a harassing purpose. Plaintiff's request for sanctions is denied.

DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Jul-27-2018 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT ARTURO DEVESA'S DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT.

Defendant Arturo Devesa's demurrer to the first through fifth causes of action in the complaint is off calendar as to the first cause of action and overruled as to the second through fifth causes of action. The first cause of action is not alleged against Mr. Devesa, so there is no basis for him to demurrer to that cause of action. The second through fifth causes of action seek to hold Mr. Devesa liable based on Mr. Devesa's own conduct and not on the basis of the alter ego doctrine, so Mr. Devesa's arguments on the grounds of the alter ego doctrine are immaterial. To the extent that the demurrer is based on lack of personal jurisdiction over Mr. Devesa, he waived the right to contest jurisdiction by filing an answer before filing this demurrer and, even if he didn't, he fails to develop any coherent argument why the court lacks personal jurisdiction over him.

Order is signed. (see scanned order for details)

Notice Of Motion And Motion To Quash Service Of Summons
Off Calendar Jun-25-2018 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT ARTURO DEVESA's Motion To Quash Service Of Summons.

Defendant Arturo Devesa's motion to quash service of summons is denied. Substituted service per CCP 415.20(b) was properly made on Mr. Devesa. Plaintiffs acted with reasonable diligence in trying to personally serve Mr. Devesa after finding out that Mr. Devesa no longer lived at his last known address by attempting service two times at the address for MedWhat.com Inc. listed on its website, a company that Mr. Davesa served as CEO, and three times at the address for Mr. Devesa's other company, Linda HealthCare Corporation Corp, listed on the company's website. Plaintiffs' subsequent substituted service at the address listed on MedWhat's website substantially complied with the statutory requirements. The office manager of the company managing the shared office space who accepted service apparently knew Mr. Devesa and her statements showed that it was more likely than not that the served papers would be delivered to Mr. Devesa. Although Mr. Devesa states that this address is a PO Box and that "ended" on May 1, 2018, the substituted service was effected on April 30, 2018 and substituted service can be effected at a private PO box. (Ellard v. Conway (2001) 94 Cal.App.4th 540, 546). Mr. Devesa has had actual notice of the case since May 14, 2018 when he retained counsel to represent him. Mr. Devesa must respond to the complaint no later than July 13, 2018.

Order is signed. (see scanned order for details)
